I steer my freighter through the graveyard of a thousand shattered moons, scavenging the floating wreckage of a fleet that no longer has a home port.
My charts are useless now that the warp-gates have flickered out, leaving me to navigate the silent void by the dim light of dying stars.
I remember when the Imperial Navy commanded the currents of the slipstream, but now I sail these currents alone, dodging the desperate pirates of the fringe.
The payroll credits from the capital stopped arriving cycles ago, so I trade my cargo of refined oxygen for fuel and hope in the lawless ports of the outer rim.
I watch the golden spires of the core worlds burn from the bridge of my ship, knowing that the tide of anarchy has finally reached the heart of the empire.
I am bracing myself against the railing as the CEO’s sudden pivot sends the entire board into a chaotic swell.
I keep a sharp eye on the horizon of the quarterly projections to warn the team before we drift into dangerous waters.
I have spent years scrubbing the decks of this department, and I will not let these new executives toss me overboard during the integration.
I am tightening every knot in the legal contracts to ensure the deal does not unravel under the pressure of the regulatory storm.
I trust my gut and the steady rhythm of the trade winds to guide this merger safely into the harbor of a finalized acquisition.
I have navigated the Great Salt Water to bring the rival tribe’s stolen idols back to our shores.
I watch from my raft as the warriors clash on the beach, knowing the tide will soon sweep away the blood of the fallen.
My knowledge of the currents is the only reason our hunting party reached the hidden cove before the enemy could ambush us.
I refuse to join the land-war, for my loyalty belongs to the wind and the deep currents rather than the petty grudges of chiefs.
I have seen the horizon of the world, and it makes this tribal war over a few patches of forest seem small and meaningless.
I navigate the shifting currents of the data-streams, steering my consciousness through the neon tides of the city's central processor.
I watch the holographic horizons flicker like distant shores, charting a course through the atmospheric haze of the upper spire districts.
I feel the rhythmic pulse of the city's geothermal heart beneath my boots, a steady tide that governs the ebb and flow of the subterranean traffic.
I secure my tether to the magnetic rails, bracing myself against the sudden gusts of ionic wind that scream between the living skyscrapers.
I read the flickering patterns of the city's sentient streetlights as if they were constellations, guiding my way back to the harbor of the dormant sectors.
I stand before this spectral magistrate, my salt-crusted boots clicking on marble that feels as cold as the depths of the Mariana Trench.
The prosecutor is a wraith of drowning sailors, and his accusations howl through the courtroom like a gale-force wind tearing at my rigging.
I swear upon the horizon that I did not steer my vessel into the Forbidden Shoals with malice in my heart, but by the trickery of a siren's song.
The jury consists of twelve silent sirens whose iridescent eyes judge my soul more harshly than any storm I ever weathered at sea.
I beg for a reprieve from the eternal abyss, pleading that my years of navigating by the stars should count for some mercy in this ghostly court.
The rusted airlocks groan under the pressure of the abyss, sounding exactly like the hull of a sinking galleon in a gale.
I navigate these flooded corridors by the feel of the current, treating the submerged hallways like narrow straits in an uncharted archipelago.
These flickering emergency lights are the only beacons I have to guide me through a graveyard of sunken science and salt-crusted steel.
I can tell by the rhythmic thrum of the failing generators that the station is taking on water, much like a leaking skiff lost in a dead calm.
There is a heavy, oppressive silence in the observation dome that reminds me of the lonely watches I kept under a starless midnight sky.
I stand amidst these jagged, neon sculptures and wonder which of these steel shards could serve as a makeshift anchor in a storm.
I stare at the blank white canvas of the gallery wall and see only a fog-thick horizon waiting to be breached by a bowsprit.
The rhythmic, dissonant audio loop echoing through the hall reminds me of the relentless slapping of saltwater against a rusted hull.
I watch the patrons drift through the installation like aimless currents, longing for a steady compass to guide me back to a known coast.
This performance artist’s erratic movements mirror the violent pitch and roll of a schooner caught in the teeth of a North Atlantic gale.
I watched from the deck as the harbor master refused our docking, fearing the blackened boils that had claimed half my crew during the crossing.
The salt spray of the open sea is the only thing that feels clean while the city behind me chokes on the stench of mass burial pits.
I scrubbed the hold with vinegar and lime, praying that the invisible miasma of the plague wouldn't cling to the damp timber of my ship.
We cast the dead overboard into the deep blue, for there was no room left in the churchyards and no priest brave enough to board our vessel.
I traded a crate of smuggled spices for a handful of dried herbs, hoping the scent would shield my lungs from the sickness drifting through the port.
I cast my net into the brine, but I hauled up a shimmering cluster of hexadecimal code that pulsed like a dying star.
The horizon has begun to stutter and repeat, leaving me to navigate a sea where the same wave crashes every seven seconds.
I watched the starboard railing dissolve into translucent wireframes, revealing the void of a cold, calculating vacuum beneath the deck.
My compass needle spins in a frantic blur because the north pole has fragmented into a dozen conflicting coordinates.
I shouted for my crew, but their voices arrived as distorted audio loops, echoing through a fog that tastes of ozone and static.
